caveats: a: Broad food-manufacturing occupations may not capture the industry's more complex assembly and formulation tasks.
a: Advanced automation includes robotics, dedicated equipment, cloud, and non-AI software.
rho: Business AI definitions recently expanded to any function.
rho: Adoption does not equal labor capture and may largely reflect office productivity.
e: No source measures eligibility in the frozen dataset population.
e: The common food-processing margin bridge may misclassify businesses with high promotion, licensing, or co-pack pass-through revenue.
s5: Neither source isolates NAICS 311412 or the stated EBITDA band.
s5: Owner plans include transfer forms that may not qualify and may never close.
q: Conagra is much larger and more brand-heavy than the target population.
q: Price/mix and promotion evidence reflects the whole commercial model, not AI savings specifically.
d5: Retail category data include products that may sit in adjacent NAICS codes.
d5: Current sales levels do not establish growth, and the base-case conversion is judgmental.
o: The code contains varied products with different insourcing risks.
o: Consolidation may remove demand from LMM operators even though the manufacturing function persists.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat finished-product and co-manufacturing sales priced per unit, case, shipment, or production run through branded, private-label, foodservice, institutional, and distributor programs.
lens.excluded_activities: Frozen seafood, dairy specialties, cakes and pastries, plain frozen fruit or vegetables, meat-only processing, captive internal plants, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market manufacturers of frozen dinners, entrees, side dishes, pizza, whipped topping, waffles, pancakes, French toast, and similar specialty foods for repeat retail, foodservice, institutional, private-label, co-manufacturing, and distributor customers.

a.bridge: BLS measures occupations across all food manufacturing rather than NAICS 311412; Census measures advanced-technology exposure across manufacturing rather than remaining AI task exposure. I map AI to demand and production planning, recipe and label documentation, vision-inspection triage, maintenance prioritization, order management, customer service, and administration, then discount cooking, batching, assembly, sanitation, packaging intervention, refrigeration, maintenance, and material movement. All-technology manufacturing exposure likely overstates AI-only exposure.
caveats: Broad food-manufacturing occupations may not capture the industry's more complex assembly and formulation tasks.
Advanced automation includes robotics, dedicated equipment, cloud, and non-AI software.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit wage-weighted occupation matrix and time studies across frozen-meal, pizza, breakfast, and co-manufacturing plants, including current planning and vision automation, would materially change the range.
a.rationale: Prepared frozen foods require more formulation, assembly, scheduling, and SKU management than single-commodity freezing, creating cognitive opportunities around a still predominantly physical production system. Food-safety accountability and line work cap substitution.

d5.bridge: Circana data reported by the Institute of Food Technologists show large current dollar categories for frozen dinners and entrees and pizza, while Conagra reports fiscal 2025 frozen price/mix pressure and foodservice volume weakness. USDA research classifies frozen entrees and pizza as convenience foods requiring minimal preparation and documents long-run convenience demand drivers. None measures five-year constant-price output for NAICS 311412. I use category scale and convenience as modest-growth supports but discount nominal prices and recent channel weakness; dollar sales likely overstate quantity growth.
caveats: Retail category data include products that may sit in adjacent NAICS codes.
Current sales levels do not establish growth, and the base-case conversion is judgmental.
d5.change_evidence: Circana or NIQ five-year unit-volume trends and constant-price forecasts for frozen meals, pizza, breakfast, sides, appetizers, and foodservice co-manufacturing, with channel and price-tier splits, would materially revise d5.
d5.rationale: Convenience, smaller households, dual-earner schedules, value relative to restaurant meals, private label, institutional demand, and product innovation support modest real growth. Health perceptions, restaurant and fresh prepared-food competition, promotion dependence, and mature freezer space create downside.

caveats: No source measures eligibility in the frozen dataset population.
The common food-processing margin bridge may misclassify businesses with high promotion, licensing, or co-pack pass-through revenue.
e.change_evidence: A record-level review of the 175 frozen candidates using ownership, normalized EBITDA, customer concentration, branded versus co-manufacturing mix, certifications, facility condition, licensing, and captive status would directly measure eligibility.
e.rationale: Most firms classified here should be operating manufacturers with repeat external customers. Deductions cover captive facilities, brand or asset structures not available for control transfer, businesses outside the target normalized EBITDA band, dependence on one retailer or licensed brand, nontransferable founder recipes or relationships, and plants with material food-safety or capital liabilities.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 311412 ($3,902,346,187) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 311412 ($24,301,895,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1525 (margins.json[31]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Food Processing EBITDA/Sales 15.25% (dominant subsector of NAICS 31)); revenue/employee $398,948 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 311412); class <5: 122 firms, avg revenue/firm $971,377 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $148,135 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 57 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,933,105 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $294,799 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 62 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,312,919 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $505,220 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 115 firms, avg revenue/firm $13,372,200 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,039,260 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 60 firms, avg revenue/firm $52,489,933 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $8,004,715 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 44 firms, avg revenue/firm $435,922,727 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $66,478,216 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 175

caveats: The code contains varied products with different insourcing risks.
Consolidation may remove demand from LMM operators even though the manufacturing function persists.
o.change_evidence: Customer make-versus-buy interviews, co-manufacturing churn, retailer commissary investments, restaurant insourcing, and substitution data across fresh prepared, shelf-stable, meal-kit, and frozen formats would test operator bypass.
o.rationale: Frozen meals and specialty foods require physical formulation, cooking, assembly, freezing, food-safety controls, packaging, traceability, cold storage, and distribution by an accountable operator. Retailers, brands, restaurants, or institutions can insource or switch to fresh and shelf-stable formats, and large manufacturers can consolidate volume, but software alone cannot supply the retained physical basket.

q.bridge: Conagra's large branded Refrigerated and Frozen segment recorded a 3.5% adverse price/mix change in fiscal 2025 primarily from higher strategic trade investment, while frozen-category competition includes retailer private labels. This is not LMM retention of AI benefits. I infer moderate retention under fixed unit pricing, then reduce it for retailer promotions, co-manufacturing rebids, foodservice negotiations, and private-label competition. Large-brand economics may overstate pricing power but promotion data may understate specialized co-manufacturer retention.
caveats: Conagra is much larger and more brand-heavy than the target population.
Price/mix and promotion evidence reflects the whole commercial model, not AI savings specifically.
q.change_evidence: Customer and co-pack contracts, trade-spend histories, bid renewals, price-cost bridges, promotion accruals, and gross margin by branded, private-label, institutional, and foodservice channel at independent firms would materially move q.
q.rationale: Operators can initially retain productivity gains because revenue is per case or run, not cost-plus labor reimbursement. Over five years, customer negotiations, trade spending, private-label price points, competing manufacturers, and shelf-space economics share much of a generalized benefit; differentiated formulations and reliable capacity preserve more.

rho.bridge: Census BTOS finds broad any-function AI use across firms, while a 2026 Census working paper reports production-of-goods AI use at only 6% and limited functional breadth among adopters. Neither measures implementation of exposed labor in frozen-food LMM plants. I extrapolate five years forward but discount food-safety validation, SKU changeovers, unreliable plant data, legacy controls, and smaller-firm integration capacity. Broad any-function use overstates plant implementation, while the current 6% production figure may understate five-year adoption.
caveats: Business AI definitions recently expanded to any function.
Adoption does not equal labor capture and may largely reflect office productivity.
rho.change_evidence: Audited before-and-after labor hours, waste, false-reject rates, downtime, and quality outcomes from AI deployments at independent frozen-meal and pizza plants would materially revise rho.
rho.rationale: Planning, documentation, service, and administrative uses can deploy relatively quickly. Machine vision, yield control, and production optimization are harder because errors can create recalls, downtime, or wasted food, so only a minority of exposed opportunity is implemented in the base case.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures stated five-year sale or transfer plans across US business owners, finding 17% for owners age 55 or older and 10% for younger owners. Project Equity reports 60% of small and midsize manufacturers have owners at or near retirement age. I blend these signals and discount gifts, internal reorganizations, closures, withdrawn processes, and failed transactions. Older manufacturing ownership likely overstates completed qualifying transfers.
caveats: Neither source isolates NAICS 311412 or the stated EBITDA band.
Owner plans include transfer forms that may not qualify and may never close.
s5.change_evidence: Closed-deal data from Axial, GF Data, BizBuySell, IBBA, and food-sector advisers filtered to frozen specialty manufacturers and $1-10M EBITDA, divided by verified eligible firms, would materially change s5.
s5.rationale: The large estimated LMM population and aging owner base create potential supply, but frozen-food deals require refrigeration and environmental diligence, customer and license transfer, working capital, and food-safety review. Those constraints keep completed control transfers below stated intentions.

principal_driver: Convenience-oriented frozen meals and co-manufacturing programs create recurring demand that still requires accountable physical production.
principal_weakness: Plant-floor labor is difficult for AI to replace, and powerful retail and foodservice customers can capture much of a generalized cost advantage.
